title: July 2026 - Liberty Street Economics
gist: This is a July 2026 Liberty Street Economics index page carrying a single post abstract: the previously documented 'overnight drift' in US equity futures — a ~3.7% annualized return earned in the 2:00–3:00 a.m. ET window as European markets open — has faded to roughly zero since 2021, and the authors use an inventory-risk framework to diagnose which channel drove the disappearance.

themes.theme: Decay of the equity-futures overnight drift anomaly

insights.claim: The 'overnight drift' — a large, persistent return from holding U.S. equity futures in the narrow 2:00–3:00 a.m. Eastern window when European equity markets open — historically generated roughly 3.7 percent per annum.
insights.speaker: Nina Boyarchenko, Lars C. Larsen, and Paul Whelan
insights.so_what: Quantifies the historical size of a well-known intraday anomaly that systematic overnight strategies were built to harvest.
insights.quote: the 2:00–3:00 window that previously generated roughly 3.7 percent per annum
insights.locator: In a 2021 Liberty Street Economics post

insights.claim: Five additional years of data show the U.S. equity-futures overnight drift in the 2:00–3:00 a.m. ET window has averaged close to zero since 2021, indicating the anomaly has faded post-publication.
insights.speaker: Nina Boyarchenko, Lars C. Larsen, and Paul Whelan
insights.so_what: A once-tradable overnight edge has decayed to zero — a textbook case of alpha eroding after being documented, a warning for anyone still running overnight-drift strategies.
insights.quote: that pattern appears to have faded
insights.locator: Five additional years of data later

insights.claim: The authors use an inventory-risk framework to attribute the drift's disappearance to one of three observable channels: the dispersion of closing order imbalances, the level of return variance, or the risk-bearing capacity of liquidity providers.
insights.speaker: Nina Boyarchenko, Lars C. Larsen, and Paul Whelan
insights.so_what: Points to the observable levers (order-imbalance dispersion, variance, dealer capacity) worth monitoring to judge whether overnight anomalies revive or persist.
insights.quote: which of three observable channels—the dispersion of closing order imbalances, the level of return variance, or the risk-bearing capacity of liquidity providers—accounts for the change
insights.locator: In this post, we revisit the overnight drift
